bonuses.leadin: In line with his philosophy of using local materials, Burkinaɓe architect Francis Kéré used this material for schools in his hometown of Gando. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this material widely used in Sudano-Sahelian architecture as well as in sukhala and tata-somba houses. The Great Mosque of Djenné is made from bricks of this material from the banks of the Niger River.
In his buildings in Gando, Kéré uses raised roofs and underground pipes for this purpose. The Eastgate Centre in Harare takes inspiration from termite mounds to achieve this purpose.

Kéré was inspired by maypoles to incorporate this design feature into Munich’s TUM Tower. Johnny Örbäck (“ER-beck”) encouraged the construction of the first skyscraper with this design feature after seeing an artist’s marble sculpture.
answers: mud [accept adobe or mudbrick; prompt on soil or dirt or silt or loam or clay by asking, “What do you get by mixing that with water?”]
passive ventilation [or natural ventilation; accept passive cooling or natural cooling; accept air circulation; accept air conditioning; accept descriptions of cooling a building]
twisting [accept torsion; accept a helix shape; accept turning; accept Turning Torso]
answers_sanitized: mud [accept adobe or mudbrick; prompt on soil or dirt or silt or loam or clay by asking, "What do you get by mixing that with water?"]
passive ventilation [or natural ventilation; accept passive cooling or natural cooling; accept air circulation; accept air conditioning; accept descriptions of cooling a building]
twisting [accept torsion; accept a helix shape; accept turning; accept Turning Torso]

parts_sanitized: Name this material widely used in Sudano-Sahelian architecture as well as in sukhala and tata-somba houses. The Great Mosque of Djenne is made from bricks of this material from the banks of the Niger River.
In his buildings in Gando, Kere uses raised roofs and underground pipes for this purpose. The Eastgate Centre in Harare takes inspiration from termite mounds to achieve this purpose.

Kere was inspired by maypoles to incorporate this design feature into Munich's TUM Tower. Johnny Orback ("ER-beck") encouraged the construction of the first skyscraper with this design feature after seeing an artist's marble sculpture.
